Hello! It’s great to meet a fellow fan of *Subnautica*. That specific brand of "thalassophobia-meets-survival" is a hard itch to scratch, but as a graphic designer, I know you likely appreciate the atmosphere, world-building, and high-stakes tension of the deep. 

Since you’re looking for that same alien ocean dread, I’ve curated a selection of films and one game that mirror the isolation, mystery, and terrifying environments you loved in *Subnautica*.

### 1. Underwater (2020)
If you loved the feeling of being trapped on the ocean floor while everything goes wrong, this is your top pick. It captures the sheer claustrophobia and "industrial horror" of a failed deep-sea facility. The creature design is fantastic—it’s visceral, fast-paced, and nails that sensation of being an intruder in an environment where you simply don't belong.

### 2. The Abyss (1989)
This is a classic for a reason. As a designer, you’ll appreciate the practical effects and the incredible sense of scale in the underwater sequences. It captures the "scientific exploration gone wrong" aspect of *Subnautica* perfectly. It balances the wonder of discovering an unknown alien presence with the crushing pressure of the deep, making it a perfect atmospheric match.

### 3. Sphere (1998)
This film nails the mystery and psychological tension of finding something "alien" on the ocean floor. It focuses on a team of specialists trapped in a high-tech underwater habitat, mirroring the research-base vibe of your game. It’s less about jump scares and more about the creeping realization that the environment—and whatever is hiding in it—is far more complex than the crew anticipated.

### 4. Leviathan (1989)
If what terrified you in *Subnautica* was the thought of deep-sea miners stumbling upon something they shouldn't have disturbed, *Leviathan* is essential viewing. It’s a gritty, creature-feature survival film that leans into the "trapped on the ocean floor" trope. It’s got that grimy, utilitarian aesthetic that fits the survivor-engineer vibe you’re used to.

### 5. Shark Attack Deathmatch 2 (2015)
Since you specifically requested a game experience to complement your movies, this is the best fit from the list. It’s a raw, indie survival experience that keeps the focus entirely on underwater dread. With multiple environments—like exploring flooded cities or shipwrecks—it taps into that same high-tension, first-person survival loop where you’re constantly looking over your shoulder to see what’s lurking in the murk.

Hopefully, these picks keep your heart rate up and provide that perfect, haunting underwater dread you’re craving! Which one are you planning to dive into first?

results.query: Dark noir city with no sun, aliens that rewrite human memory, detective wakes with no past
results.llm_response:
Hello! As a fellow creative, I know exactly the kind of atmosphere you're craving. You want that high-contrast, moody aesthetic—the kind where the visual storytelling is just as important as the mystery itself. Given your love for atmospheric sci-fi, horror, and the "no-sun" noir aesthetic, I’ve curated five picks that perfectly capture that feeling of a fractured reality and the struggle for lost identity.

Here are my top recommendations for your next deep dive:

### 1. Dark City (1998)
This is an absolute must-watch for you. It’s the gold standard for what you described: a sunless, ever-changing urban nightmare ruled by beings who literally manipulate the architecture and the memories of the city's inhabitants. As a graphic designer, you’ll appreciate the stunning, brutalist-meets-gothic production design. It hits that "detective with no past" trope perfectly while leaning into the surreal sci-fi horror you enjoy.

### 2. Strange Days (1995)
Since you enjoy tech-noir and dystopias, this is a perfect fit. It captures a gritty, hyper-real vision of Los Angeles at the end of the millennium, complete with a dark conspiracy involving memories—or "clips"—that people can experience firsthand. It’s dark, paranoid, and leans heavily into the cyberpunk aesthetic that often informs great design work.

### 3. Night Catcher (2019)
I’ve included this indie game because it mirrors your exact scenario: a cop wakes up in his office with no clear grasp of his situation, hunted by a psychopath and trapped in a hostile environment. It’s an atmospheric survival experience that prioritizes tension and isolation. Its indie nature means the focus is heavily on the mood and the "trapped" feeling you seem to be looking for.

### 4. Alphaville (1965)
If you want to see where the "future noir" genre truly found its footing, look no further. It features a private eye investigating a city on another planet where emotion and memory are under threat by a cold, calculating machine. It’s a beautifully stylized piece of cinema—its stark, high-contrast cinematography is legendary and will likely provide some fantastic visual inspiration for your own design work.

### 5. Nightmare Detective (2007)
Since you mentioned a love for horror and mysteries that blur the lines of reality, this film is a surreal voyage into the subconscious. It features a police detective who has the ability to enter the dreams of others to solve gruesome crimes. It’s a dark, visually arresting, and unsettling investigation that explores the fragility of the human psyche—exactly the kind of "weird mystery" that fits your preferences.

Enjoy getting lost in these dark, sunless worlds!

results.query: Top-down co-op magic game — Scandinavian mythology, friendly fire spells, chaotic fun
results.llm_response:
Hello! As a fellow software engineer, I know exactly what you’re looking for: that perfect balance of high-stakes mechanical depth, atmospheric world-building, and the kind of "chaotic fun" that only happens when you accidentally (or intentionally) blast your teammates in a co-op session. 

Given your preference for Scandinavian mythology and action-heavy RPGs—and your interest in those hilarious moments of friendly fire—I’ve curated five top-down titles that fit your criteria perfectly:

### 1. Magicka
This is the gold standard for your request. It’s a top-down, co-op magic game drenched in Scandinavian mythology. As a developer, you’ll appreciate the spell-casting system: it doesn't use traditional classes but instead gives you a complex, elemental combination engine. The "chaotic fun" here is legendary; because friendly fire is always on and your spells interact dynamically with the environment, you will inevitably end up nuking your friends as much as your enemies. It’s exactly the kind of technical, high-chaos experience you’re looking for.

### 2. Gunnheim
If you want something a bit punchier with that top-down perspective, *Gunnheim* is a perfect fit. It blends Viking mythology with a twin-stick shooter aesthetic. The game actively encourages "wicked" friendly fire kills, making it a riot for local or online co-op sessions. It’s less about puzzles and more about pure, visceral carnage—perfect for blowing off steam after a long day of coding.

### 3. Pagan Online
While this draws more from Slavic than Scandinavian myth, it hits every other requirement on your list with high marks. It’s a fast-paced, top-down Action RPG with tight WASD controls that will feel intuitive to a developer. The combat is satisfyingly brutal, and the hero-based system offers eight distinct "flavors" of gameplay, providing the mechanical variety you likely crave in an RPG.

### 4. Rune (2000)
For a deeper dive into the Norse aesthetic, this classic is a must-play. It’s a third-person, action-heavy experience that leans heavily into the Ragnarok mythos. You can align with gods like Thor or Odin, and the game features a heavy focus on pillaging and combat-oriented progression. It’s a bit older, but it delivers the exact grim, dark fantasy atmosphere you enjoy.

### 5. R.A.W. Realms of Ancient War
This title offers a classic top-down, hack-and-slash experience that emphasizes co-op play. It’s a straightforward, satisfying action RPG where you can choose between different classes (including a Mage) to tear through hordes of enemies. It’s a great, uncomplicated "pick-up-and-play" experience that lets you and your friends focus on the teamwork aspect of managing different elemental or magical abilities in a fantasy setting.

All of these selections avoid the genres you dislike, focusing purely on the action, adventure, and atmospheric mechanics that make for a great gaming session. Enjoy the chaos!

results.query: Dark fantasy Arthurian RPG — you are Mordred, King Arthur died, the island of Avalon, undead
results.llm_response:
Hello there! Given your background as a software engineer and your deep appreciation for dark, atmospheric RPGs, I’ve curated a selection that perfectly aligns with your fascination for the Arthurian mythos—specifically those gritty, complex narratives where you step into the shoes of the "villain" or navigate a shattered, undead version of Avalon.

Here are the five titles I believe you will find most compelling:

### 1. King Arthur: Knight's Tale
This is the definitive answer to your request. You play as Sir Mordred, resurrected by the Lady of the Lake to finish the job you started: killing King Arthur. As a developer, you’ll likely appreciate the mechanical depth of its hybrid tactical-RPG system. It treats "choices" as systemic variables—your moral compass (Christianity vs. Old Faith) and your management of Camelot directly impact the branching narrative and your heroes' loyalty. It’s dark, tactical, and uncompromising.

### 2. Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon
If you want total immersion in a decaying Arthurian landscape, this is it. It’s an open-world, first-person RPG that captures a truly oppressive, dark fantasy atmosphere. You are not the "chosen one"—you are an underdog in a land where the magic (the Wyrdness) is actively twisting reality. Its focus on non-linear narrative and meaningful consequence-driven world-building makes it a standout for someone who values rich, complex storytelling over hand-holding.

### 3. King Arthur: Legion IX
This recent addition to the series offers a unique perspective within the same dark Avalon setting. You lead a Roman tribune through a realm corrupted by death. Given your preference for RPG/Strategy hybrids, the tactical layer here is robust and plays beautifully with the "undead/nightmare" aesthetic of the island. It’s a tighter, more focused experience that leans heavily into the dark fantasy elements you enjoy.

### 4. Arthurian Legends
Sometimes you just want to experience the visceral reality of a setting. This is a first-person action game that puts you right on the front lines of a crumbling, mythic Albion. It leans into the "undead" aspect of your request, tasking you with battling demonic beasts and the restless dead. It’s straightforward, atmospheric, and technically satisfying for when you want to bypass the grand strategy and get straight into the combat.

### 5. Sword Legacy: Omen
This title offers a "dark twist" on the classic myth, focusing on a broken, decadent Britannia before Arthur’s golden age. It features a tactical combat system that emphasizes party positioning and synergy, which should appeal to your analytical side. The art style—a fusion of gothic comics and classic animation—creates a distinct, bleak tone that fits perfectly within the dark fantasy sub-genre you prefer.

I recommend starting with **King Arthur: Knight's Tale** for the most direct hit on your specific prompt, and moving to **Tainted Grail** if you want to explore a more immersive, open-ended version of that dying world. Enjoy your descent into Avalon!

results.query: Neo-noir crime anthology film — black and white, comic book style, multiple brutal stories in one city
results.llm_response:
Hello! Given your eye for design and your love for dark, atmospheric, and stylized storytelling, I’ve curated a selection of noir-soaked experiences that lean into that high-contrast, graphic aesthetic you’re looking for. Since you enjoy horror and survival themes with a gritty edge, these picks should hit that sweet spot perfectly.

Here are my top recommendations for you:

### 1. Sin City (2005) – The Gold Standard
This is an absolute must-watch for a graphic designer. It is essentially a comic book brought to life; the stark, high-contrast black-and-white cinematography punctuated by selective splashes of color is a masterclass in visual composition. Its anthology structure, brutal tone, and hard-boiled atmosphere make it the definitive answer to your request.

### 2. Calvino Noir (2015) – The Interactive Experience
Since you enjoy atmospheric indie games, *Calvino Noir* is perfect for you. It’s a stealth-adventure game that feels like a playable film noir. The art direction is heavily influenced by architectural noir and the "shades of grey" moral ambiguity of the genre. As a designer, you will likely appreciate how the game leans into its visual identity as a core part of the gameplay mechanics.

### 3. Dark City (1998) – The Sci-Fi Atmosphere
This film captures that perfect "tech-noir" vibe you enjoy. It’s a beautifully realized, nightmarish dystopia with a unique, retro-futuristic aesthetic. It excels at building a sense of paranoia and mystery, and its distinct visual style is consistently cited as a precursor to some of the greatest sci-fi cinematography of the last 20 years.

### 4. Noir Chronicles: City of Crime (2018) – The Deep Dive
If you want to spend more time in a gritty, metropolitan mystery, this game offers a classic point-and-click investigative experience. It is intentionally inspired by classic noir tropes, focusing on a deep, mature intrigue. Its emphasis on atmosphere and investigation makes it an excellent choice if you’re looking for something you can sit down and immerse yourself in.

### 5. Metropolis (1927) – The Visual Foundation
While it predates the modern "noir" label, I’ve included this because of its historical significance to the visual language of dystopia and urban despair. It is a masterpiece of German Expressionism. The use of shadow, brutalist architecture, and contrast has influenced almost every film on your list. For a designer, seeing how they created such complex, atmospheric depth in 1927 is incredibly rewarding.

Enjoy diving into these dark, visually striking worlds!
